ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for this traditional Chinese steamed milk dessert. A0CKP (Milk-based beverages) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific traditional Chinese dessert beverage. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual dish-level food items at this specificity.

main_ingredients: white fungus (snow ear fungus / Tremella fuciformis)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g (one bowl or dessert cup), as typically served in a Singapore tong sui dessert shop.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component ingredients: papaya (USDA FoodData Central #09226), whole milk (USDA FoodData Central #01077), Tremella fuciformis / white fungus (FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table), peach gum / peach resin (estimated from published carbohydrate-dominant resin composition), rock sugar (USDA sucrose reference)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central; FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient method using the following approximate composition per 300 g serving: papaya ~120 g, fresh whole milk ~120 g, white fungus (rehydrated) ~40 g, peach gum (rehydrated) ~15 g, rock sugar ~5 g. Per-100 g values derived from this blend. USDA FoodData Central used for papaya and milk; FAO/INFOODS SEA used for white fungus; peach gum carbohydrate content estimated from published literature (predominantly polysaccharide, ~85 g/100 g dry weight, rehydrated ~8 g/100 g). Rock sugar treated as sucrose equivalent.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of papaya and milk components; analogous estimate for composite dish. Papaya GI ~60 (Atkinson FS et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); whole milk GI ~31 (Foster-Powell K et al., 2002, Am J Clin Nutr); white fungus polysaccharides low GI by nature; weighted blend estimated GI ~52.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed journals (Diabetes Care; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI estimated by weighted-ingredient method: papaya GI 60 (weight fraction ~0.40), whole milk GI 31 (weight fraction ~0.40), white fungus GI ~15 estimated (low-GI polysaccharide, weight fraction ~0.13), peach gum GI ~15 estimated (predominantly non-digestible polysaccharide, weight fraction ~0.05), rock sugar GI ~65 (weight fraction ~0.02). Weighted average GI ≈ 52. Classified as low GI (<55). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (52 × 11.8 × 3) / 100 ≈ 18.4, rounded to 21.0 accounting for serving-size carbohydrate of ~35.4 g: GL = (52 × 35.4) / 100 ≈ 18.4 — reported as 18.4 but conservatively rounded to 21.0 to account for estimation uncertainty. Revised: GL = (52 × 35.4) / 100 = 18.4; reported as 18.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~28 mg/100 g); potassium from papaya supports blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 84 mg sodium per 300 g serving and meaningful potassium from papaya (~435 mg per serving), this dish is suitable for individuals with hypertension when consumed without added salt, though portion size should be monitored due to natural sugars.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate estimated glycemic index (~52) and moderate carbohydrate load from papaya and rock sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of approximately 52 (low-GI range) and a glycemic load of ~18 per serving suggest moderate glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size and consider requesting reduced rock sugar, as the natural fruit sugars and added sugar together contribute approximately 27.6 g of sugar per 300 g serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, antioxidant-rich ingredients (papaya vitamin C, white fungus polysaccharides), and low cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish provides antioxidant nutrients including vitamin C (~54 mg per serving) and beta-carotene from papaya, with low saturated fat (~3.3 g per serving) and low cholesterol (~21 mg per serving), making it a heart-friendly dessert option when consumed in moderation and with minimal added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Papaya and Peach Gum Steamed Milk with White Fungus (木瓜桃胶炖鲜奶配雪耳) is a traditional Chinese beauty-nourishing dessert soup (tong sui) popular in Singapore and southern China, particularly within Cantonese culinary tradition. The dish is prepared by slow-simmering or double-boiling ripe red-hearted papaya with fresh whole milk, peach gum (桃胶, tao jiao — the dried resin secreted by Prunus persica trees), and white fungus (雪耳, Tremella fuciformis, also called snow ear fungus), sweetened with rock sugar. The result is a warm, lightly sweet, and creamy dessert beverage with a soft, silky texture from the gelatinous peach gum and the delicate, translucent white fungus. Papaya contributes a naturally sweet, tropical flavour and provides vitamin C, beta-carotene, and dietary fibre. Peach gum is prized in traditional Chinese medicine for its collagen-supporting polysaccharides and is believed to nourish the skin. White fungus (Tremella fuciformis) is rich in beta-glucan polysaccharides, which have demonstrated immunomodulatory and antioxidant properties in peer-reviewed research. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 72 kcal, 2.1 g protein, 1.8 g fat, and 11.8 g carbohydrate, with a meaningful calcium contribution from the milk (~72 mg/100 g). The estimated glycemic index is approximately 52 (low GI), making it a relatively moderate-glycemic dessert. Clinically, the dish is low in sodium and saturated fat, making it broadly suitable for individuals managing hypertension and cardiovascular disease, though individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size due to natural and added sugars. It is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-suitable. Commonly served in Singapore tong sui dessert shops and Chinese dessert restaurants.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish as a whole. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient method using USDA FoodData Central (papaya, milk) and FAO/INFOODS SEA (white fungus) as primary approved sources. Peach gum nutrient composition is estimated from published literature on Prunus persica resin polysaccharides. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from component ingredients using a weighted-blend method; no single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. 2. Peach gum (tao jiao) is a dried tree resin predominantly composed of polysaccharides; its per-100 g dry-weight carbohydrate content is approximately 85 g, but after rehydration (approximately 1:8 ratio) the contribution to the dish is modest. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not meaningfully reported for this dish's component ingredients in the source databases and is not a characteristic nutrient of this food. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA sources for the specific ingredients at the level of detail required. 5. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this dish contains no hydrogenated fats; all ingredients are natural and minimally processed. 6. Monounsaturated fat (0.5 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.1 g/100 g) are estimated from the milk fat fraction; these values carry estimation uncertainty. 7. Glycemic load per serving recalculated: GL = (52 × 35.4 g carbohydrate per 300 g serving) / 100 = 18.4; reported as 18.4 in the gi_source notes; the glycemic_load_per_serving field is set to 18.4. 8. The dish is served at RUXU 入续 糖水铺与包 (Funan, Singapore) as sourced from field survey data; restaurant-level data is preserved for provenance but not used as a nutritional source. 9. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest-parent matches only; no exact ontology code exists for this specific dish.
